rulings.Case Title: MEGAN YORK VS. JACK G. LEE ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiff Megal York To Attend Deposition Provide Testimony And Produce Docuents And Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, April 16 2019, Line 1, DEFENDANT NORMAN CHARLES CONSTRUCTION, INC.'s Motion To Compel Plaintiff Megal York To Attend Deposition Provide Testimony And Produce Docuents And Monetary Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Jason Yurasek, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendant Norman Charles Construction, Inc.'s Motion to Compel Plaintiff Megan York to Attend Deposition, Provide Testimony and Produce Documents is GRANTED. Ms. York's deposition shall occur on or before May 17, 2019. The request for monetary sanctions in the amount of $2,777.20 is granted. The prevailing party to submit a proposed order for signature by the Court. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to jyjudgeprotem@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: JOHN DG VS. DOE 1 ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Protective Order
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, April 16 2019, Line 2, OTHER COE 1's Motion For Protective Order. Pro Tem Judge Jason Yurasek, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion for Protective Order Granted in Part and Denied in Part. The motion is granted to the extent discovery seeks the actual report or reports made by the responding party as mandated by Cal. Penal Code 11166, et seq. The motion is denied to the extent discovery seeks responsive information to discovery regardless of whether it may be contained in any of said reports. The fact that discoverable information in the possession of a responding party may also be contained in a report made pursuant to Cal. Penal Code 11166, et seq. does not thereafter magically transform what was once discoverable into something that may never be seen or heard of again. The prevailing party to submit a proposed order for signature by the Court. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to jyjudgeprotem@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: RICHARD SPENCER VS. GENERAL MOTORS LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Compliance With The Courts October 25, 2018 Discovery Order And For Prospective Sanctions Of 500 Per Day
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, April 16 2019, Line 3, PLAINTIFF RICHARD SPENCER's Motion To Compel Compliance With The Courts October 25, 2018 Discovery Order And For Prospective Sanctions Of 500 Per Day. Pro Tem Judge Jason Yurasek, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion to Compel Compliance with Court's October 25, 2018 Discovery Order (the "Order") and for Prospective Sanctions of $500 Per Day is Granted as modified below. Defendant shall have until April 22, 2019 to certify in writing under oath its compliance with each discovery request subject the Order by, at a minimum, identifying each document by Bates number or other discovery material by specific identification that responds to each said discovery. After April 22, 2019, to the extent Defendant does not certify compliance with each and every of the discovery requests subject to the Order, prospective sanctions of $500 per day will accrue until such time defendant has complied with said discovery requests. The prevailing party to submit a proposed order for signature by the Court. rulings.Case Title: SANDY LIM VS. AMERICAN GENERAL LIFE INSURANCE COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Sandy Lim'S Second Motion To Compel Defendant American General Life Insurance Companys Person(S) Most Knowledgeable For Deposition And For Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, April 16, 2019, Line 7, Plaintiff Sandy Lim'S Second Motion To Compel Defendant American General Life Insurance Companys Person(S) Most Knowledgeable For Deposition And For Monetary Sanctions Plaintiff Sandy Lim's motion to compel Defendant American General Life Insurance Company's person most knowledgeable for deposition and for monetary sanctions is denied. American General, at Plaintiff's request, properly designated Angela Butler as the person most knowledgeable to testify on its behalf in response to Plaintiff's PMK notice. It did not refuse to make Ms. Butler available for deposition, but rather was forced to reschedule her deposition due to a personal family emergency on her part. The Court has reviewed in camera Ms. Butler's declaration, which substantiates Defendant's position. It appears to the Court from the information submitted to it in camera that American General will not be in a position to make Ms. Butler available for a PMK deposition before April 26 at the earliest. American General should be prepared to address whether it intends to designate an alternative PMK witness and, if so, when that person's deposition may be taken in San Francisco, as required by Code of Civil Procedure section 2025.250(b). In addition, the parties should be prepared to address whether any continuance of the existing summary judgment schedule and trial date will be required. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for American General is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: BAOYUAN CHEN ET AL VS. S.N. SANDS CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, April 16, 2019, Line 5, DEFENDANTs S.N. SANDS CORPORATION and SCOTT PATRICK's MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT (part 1 of 2 for tentative ruling). Defendants S.N. Sands Corporation dba S&S Trucking and Scott Patrick's motion to strike portions of Plaintiff's complaint is granted. A motion to strike is a proper procedural vehicle to strike inadequately pled punitive damages allegations. (See Turman v. Turning Point of Central California, Inc. (2010) 191 Cal.App.4th 53, 63-64.) In order to state a prima facie case for punitive damages, a complaint must set forth the elements stated in the general punitive damages statute, Civil Code section 3294, including allegations that the defendant has been guilty of oppression, fraud, or malice. (Civ. Code § 3294(a).) "Malice" is defined as conduct "intended by the defendant to cause injury to plaintiff, or despicable conduct that is carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others." (Civ. Code § 3294(c)(1).) Absent an intent to injure the plaintiff, malice requires more than a "willful and conscious" disregard of the plaintiff's interests; the additional component of "despicable conduct" must be found. (College Hospital, Inc. v. Superior Court (1994) 8 Cal.4th 704, 725.) Punitive damages are appropriate only if the defendant's actions are reprehensible, fraudulent, or in blatant violation of law or policy. The mere carelessness or ignorance of the defendant does not justify the imposition of punitive damages. Punitive damages are appropriate only when the tortious conduct rises to levels of extreme indifference to the plaintiff's rights, a level which decent citizens should not have to tolerate. (Pacific Gas and Electric Company v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1150, 1170-76.) tentative ruling continued on part 2 of 2. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: BAOYUAN CHEN ET AL VS. S.N. SANDS CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants S.N. Sands Corporation And Scott Patrick'S Motion To Strike Complaint (Part 2 Of 2 For Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, April 16, 2019, Line 5, Defendants S.N. Sands Corporation And Scott Patrick'S Motion To Strike Complaint (Part 2 Of 2 For Tentative Ruling Only) This action arises out of a motor vehicle accident in which the Plaintiff, a pedestrian, was run over by a truck operated by Defendants. However, the allegations in the complaint are insufficient to state a cognizable claim for punitive damages. (See Lackner v. North (2006) 135 Cal.App.4th 1188, 1212 ["Plaintiff has not cited any cases involving a collision where the court found the defendant's conduct was despicable, and we have found no California cases on point."].) At most, Plaintiff's allegations show "mere carelessness or ignorance, which is insufficient to establish malice." (Pacific Gas and Electric Company, 24 Cal.App.5th at 1171.) In Lackner v. North, the court held that the conduct of a snowboarder who struck a skier while she was standing in a flat area on a ski slope was not despicable so as to support a punitive damages claim, even if the snowboarder was reckless in travelling at high speed in a rest area. (135 Cal.App.4th at 1209-13.) The court discussed with approval an out-of- state decision in which a court held that a driver who was speeding and was not paying attention to his driving, resulting in his rear-ending plaintiff's car while it was stopped at a four-way intersection, was not subject to an award of punitive damages. (Id. at 1212-13, discussing Mayfield v. Johnson (Miss. 1967) 202 So.2d 630.) The same result follows here. Seimon v. Southern Pacific Transportation Co. (1977) 67 Cal.App.3d 600, on which Plaintiff relies, is distinguishable, as there the defendant railroad company failed to take any action to protect a crossing with crossing gates or flashing lights, despite its knowledge that the crossing was "highly dangerous" and had resulted in prior accidents. Seimon also predated the 1987 amendment of section 3294 to require a showing of despicable conduct. (See Lackner, 135 Cal.App.4th at 1211-12.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)
